text: Linus Torvalds created the Linux kernel in 1991 in Helsinki, Finland. He still maintains the project as benevolent dictator for life.
Guido van Rossum released Python in 1991 in Amsterdam. He stepped down as Python's BDFL in 2018.
Bjarne Stroustrup designed C++ at Bell Labs starting in 1979 as an extension of the C language.
Dennis Ritchie created C at Bell Labs in 1972. He also co-authored the Unix operating system with Ken Thompson.
James Gosling designed Java at Sun Microsystems, with the first public release in 1995.
Brendan Eich wrote the first version of JavaScript in 10 days at Netscape in 1995.
Anders Hejlsberg led the design of C# at Microsoft and previously created Turbo Pascal.
Yukihiro Matsumoto, known as Matz, released Ruby in 1995 in Japan.
Rasmus Lerdorf created PHP in 1994, originally for tracking visits to his online resume.
John McCarthy designed Lisp at MIT in 1958 and coined the term artificial intelligence.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989, including HTTP, HTML, and the first web browser.
Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn designed the TCP/IP protocols in the 1970s, forming the foundation of the modern Internet.
Larry Page and Sergey Brin founded Google at Stanford in 1998, building on their PageRank algorithm.
Steve Wozniak designed the Apple I personal computer in Cupertino in 1976, which Steve Jobs sold.
Bill Gates and Paul Allen founded Microsoft in 1975 in Albuquerque, New Mexico.
Mark Zuckerberg launched Facebook from his Harvard dorm room in 2004.
Jeff Bezos founded Amazon in 1994 in Seattle, initially as an online bookstore.
Elon Musk founded SpaceX in 2002 with the goal of making humanity multi-planetary.
Jack Dorsey co-founded Twitter in 2006 and later founded the payments company Square.
Reed Hastings co-founded Netflix in 1997 in Scotts Valley, California.
Linda Liukas created the children's coding book series Hello Ruby, inspired by Yukihiro Matsumoto's Ruby language.
Margaret Hamilton led the team that wrote the on-board flight software for the Apollo missions at MIT in the 1960s.
Grace Hopper developed the first compiler, A-0, at Remington Rand in 1952 and contributed to COBOL.
Ada Lovelace wrote the first computer algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in 1843.
Alan Turing proposed the universal computing machine in his 1936 paper at Cambridge, founding theoretical computer science.
Marie Curie discovered polonium and radium in Paris in 1898 and won two Nobel Prizes in different sciences.
Albert Einstein published the theory of special relativity in 1905 while working at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern.
Isaac Newton formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation at Cambridge in 1687.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, proposing evolution by natural selection after his Beagle voyage.
Rosalind Franklin produced the X-ray diffraction images that revealed DNA's structure at King's College London in 1952.
Niels Bohr developed the model of atomic structure with quantized electron orbits in Copenhagen in 1913.
Werner Heisenberg formulated the uncertainty principle in 1927, a cornerstone of quantum mechanics.
Erwin Schrodinger published the wave equation that describes quantum systems in 1926 in Zurich.
Richard Feynman developed quantum electrodynamics and shared the 1965 Nobel Prize for that work.
Stephen Hawking proposed that black holes emit radiation, now called Hawking radiation, in 1974 at Cambridge.
Galileo Galilei used a telescope to discover moons of Jupiter in 1610 and supported the heliocentric model.
Johannes Kepler discovered the three laws of planetary motion in the early 17th century in Prague.
Dmitri Mendeleev published the periodic table of elements in 1869 in Saint Petersburg.
Linus Pauling won Nobel Prizes in both Chemistry and Peace, the only person to win two unshared Nobel Prizes.
Gregor Mendel established the laws of inheritance through experiments with pea plants in the 1860s at his monastery in Brno.
Louis Pasteur developed pasteurization and the first rabies vaccine in 19th-century France.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin at St. Mary's Hospital in London in 1928, launching the antibiotic era.
Jonas Salk developed the inactivated polio vaccine in 1955 at the University of Pittsburgh.
Rachel Carson published Silent Spring in 1962, sparking the modern environmental movement.
Jane Goodall began her landmark study of wild chimpanzees in Gombe, Tanzania in 1960.
Stephen Jay Gould proposed the theory of punctuated equilibrium with Niles Eldredge in 1972.
Carl Sagan popularized astronomy through the Cosmos TV series in 1980 and advised NASA on planetary missions.
Katherine Johnson computed orbital mechanics for NASA's early space missions including John Glenn's first orbital flight in 1962.
Vera Rubin's observations of galaxy rotation provided early evidence for dark matter in the 1970s.
Barbara McClintock discovered genetic transposition in maize and won the Nobel Prize in 1983 for that work.
Abraham Lincoln signed the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863, freeing slaves in Confederate states.
George Washington commanded the Continental Army during the American Revolution and became the first U.S. president in 1789.
Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France in 1804 and was defeated at Waterloo in 1815.
Winston Churchill served as British Prime Minister during most of World War II and won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1953.
Franklin D. Roosevelt was elected to four terms as U.S. president and led the country through the Great Depression and World War II.
Mahatma Gandhi led India to independence from British rule in 1947 through nonviolent civil disobedience.
Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 27 years before becoming South Africa's first democratically elected president in 1994.
Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his I Have a Dream speech at the 1963 March on Washington.
Queen Elizabeth I reigned over England from 1558 to 1603, presiding over the Elizabethan era.
Catherine the Great expanded the Russian Empire during her reign from 1762 to 1796.
Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon in 49 BC, triggering civil war and the end of the Roman Republic.
Genghis Khan founded the Mongol Empire in 1206 and became the largest contiguous land empire in history.
Cleopatra VII was the last active pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt and died in 30 BC.
Joan of Arc led French troops to several victories during the Hundred Years' War before her execution in 1431.
Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492 while seeking a westward route to Asia.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of Earth from 1519 to 1522, though Magellan died en route.
Marco Polo traveled to China in the 13th century and described his journeys in The Travels of Marco Polo.
Vasco da Gama led the first European maritime voyage to India in 1498.
Eleanor Roosevelt chaired the UN committee that drafted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, adopted in 1948.
Susan B. Anthony was a leader of the U.S. women's suffrage movement in the 19th century.
Frederick Douglass escaped slavery and became a leading abolitionist and statesman in 19th-century America.
Harriet Tubman helped enslaved people escape via the Underground Railroad and served as a Union scout during the Civil War.
Malala Yousafzai survived an attack by the Taliban and won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2014 at age 17.
Mother Teresa founded the Missionaries of Charity in 1950 and won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979.
Lech Walesa led the Polish Solidarity movement in the 1980s and became Poland's first democratically elected president.
William Shakespeare wrote Hamlet, Macbeth, and many other plays in late 16th-century and early 17th-century England.
Jane Austen published Pride and Prejudice in 1813, one of several novels examining English gentry life.
Leo Tolstoy wrote War and Peace and Anna Karenina in 19th-century Russia.
Fyodor Dostoevsky wrote Crime and Punishment and The Brothers Karamazov, exploring moral and existential themes.
Gabriel Garcia Marquez wrote One Hundred Years of Solitude in 1967, defining magical realism in Latin American literature.
Toni Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993; her novel Beloved received the Pulitzer Prize.
Virginia Woolf wrote Mrs Dalloway and To the Lighthouse, pioneering stream-of-consciousness narration.
James Joyce wrote Ulysses in 1922, considered a landmark of modernist literature.
Franz Kafka wrote The Metamorphosis in 1915; most of his work was published posthumously by Max Brod.
Ernest Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in 1954 and is known for novels like The Old Man and the Sea.
Pablo Picasso co-founded Cubism with Georges Braque and painted Guernica in 1937 in response to the Spanish Civil War.
Vincent van Gogh painted The Starry Night in 1889 while staying at an asylum in Saint-Remy-de-Provence.
Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa around 1503 in Florence; he was also an inventor and anatomist.
Michelangelo sculpted the David in 1504 and painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling between 1508 and 1512.
Frida Kahlo painted intense self-portraits drawing on Mexican folk art in the early 20th century.
Claude Monet led the Impressionist movement and painted the Water Lilies series at his Giverny garden.
Georgia O'Keeffe painted large-scale flowers and New Mexico landscapes in the 20th century.
Salvador Dali was a key figure in Surrealism; his painting The Persistence of Memory features melting clocks.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart composed over 600 works including operas like The Magic Flute, dying young in 1791.
Ludwig van Beethoven composed nine symphonies and continued composing despite progressive hearing loss.
Johann Sebastian Bach composed the Brandenburg Concertos and Mass in B minor in 18th-century Germany.
Bob Dylan won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016, the first songwriter to receive the award.
The Beatles formed in Liverpool in 1960 and changed the course of popular music in the 1960s.
Aretha Franklin was crowned the Queen of Soul and won 18 Grammy Awards over her career.
Akira Kurosawa directed films like Seven Samurai and Rashomon, influencing global cinema in the 20th century.
